widget.title: Nearby Activity
widget.noActivity: No nearby activity detected — all clear

neighbor.noDirectImpact: No direct impact on your parcels identified
neighbor.noActivitiesHint: Try increasing the search radius or removing filters

mitigation.windthrow: Create gradual wind buffer on exposed edge. Plant fast-growing birch/alder mix in 15 m strip. Monitor for windthrow after storms.
mitigation.beetleSpread: Increase monitoring frequency on your spruce stands. Set pheromone traps along the nearest boundary. Remove any infested trees within 3 weeks.
mitigation.drainageChange: Monitor soil moisture in downstream stands. Check for waterlogging changes in spring. Consider drainage improvements if needed.
mitigation.edgeExposure: Thin edge trees gradually over 2-3 years to create aerodynamic profile. Avoid abrupt clear-edges facing prevailing wind.
mitigation.accessChange: Check if new road improves your timber transport options. Contact the local road association about shared access rights.
mitigation.regionalAwareness: Stay informed through Skogsstyrelsen's regional reports. No direct action needed but monitor developments.

demo.fellingWestDesc: Avverkningsanmälan filed — 4.5 ha clearcut planned 800 m west
demo.fellingWestExplanation: New clearcut 800 m west creates an exposed forest edge. Prevailing SW winds will now hit Stand 3 directly — windthrow risk significantly increased for your mature spruce.
demo.fellingWestAction: Install wind monitoring on western boundary of Stand 3. Consider edge-feathering thinning to reduce wind load on exposed trees.
demo.clearcutNorthDesc: Clearcut detected via satellite — 8.2 ha, 2.1 km north
demo.clearcutNorthExplanation: Fresh clearcut creates a potential bark beetle dispersal corridor. Beetles emerging from stumps and slash may reach your spruce stands within the flight range.
demo.clearcutNorthAction: Set pheromone traps along the northern boundary. Increase monitoring frequency on Stand 1 spruce during May-August flight period.
demo.roadSouthExplanation: New forest road 1.5 km south may improve timber transport access for your Ekbacken parcels. Low negative impact — primarily positive for logistics.
demo.roadSouthAction: Contact the local road association to check if you can negotiate shared access rights for improved timber transport.
demo.plantingEastExplanation: New planting 3 km east is positive for regional forest cover. No impact on your parcels — too far for direct effects.
demo.beetleNEDesc: Bark beetle damage confirmed — 2.3 ha, 1.8 km northeast
demo.beetleNEExplanation: Active bark beetle infestation 1.8 km northeast is within the typical flight dispersal range (2-3 km). Your spruce stands in Stand 2 face elevated risk during the swarming season.
demo.beetleNEAction: URGENT: Set pheromone traps immediately. Conduct ground inspection of Stand 2 spruce for bore dust and entry holes. Report to Skogsstyrelsen if damage found.
demo.stormNWDesc: Storm damage reported — 12 ha, 4 km northwest
demo.stormNWExplanation: Storm damage 4 km northwest indicates regional wind event impact. While too far for direct exposure to your parcels, it signals elevated regional storm risk patterns.
demo.fellingSouthExplanation: Felling notification 5.2 km south is too far for any direct impact on your parcels. No action needed.
demo.thinningEastExplanation: Thinning 600 m east temporarily opens canopy and creates new forest edge exposure. Your Stand 5 in Ekbacken may experience increased wind and light exposure on its eastern boundary.
demo.thinningEastAction: Monitor Stand 5 eastern boundary for windthrow signs after storms. Consider complementary edge thinning to reduce abrupt transition.

tour.welcomeDesc: Let us show you around the platform. This quick tour will help you get the most out of your forest intelligence dashboard.
tour.healthDesc: This is the heart of your dashboard. It shows an overall health score for your forest based on satellite data, pest risk, and species diversity.
tour.timberDesc: Here you see your forest's estimated timber value with species breakdown, risk analysis, and scenario modeling.
tour.mapDesc: Explore your forest on the map. View parcels, NDVI layers, risk zones, and satellite imagery all in one place.
tour.companionDesc: Ask the AI companion anything about your forest, pest management, regulations, or forestry best practices.
tour.alertsDesc: Alerts keep you updated on bark beetle risk, weather events, NDVI changes, and regulatory deadlines.
tour.fieldModeDesc: Field Mode for work in the forest. Pre-caches data for offline use, enables GPS-tagged photo capture and field notes.
tour.completeDesc: You are ready to start monitoring your forest. Explore the dashboard, check your parcels, and ask the AI companion if you need help.
tour.settingsDesc: Take a guided tour of the platform features.
tour.completed: You have completed the tour previously.

forestProfile.heroDescription: You have a {{area}} ha {{forestType}} forest, mostly {{dominantSpecies}} around {{ageYears}} years old, on {{soilType}} soil in {{county}}. Here's what that means for you.
forestProfile.demoNote: Showing sample data — connect your parcels for real insights

speciesDescription.spruce: Your spruce makes up {{percentage}}% of your forest. At around {{age}} years, these trees are building volume steadily and will be ready for final harvest in about 25 years.
speciesDescription.pine: Pine accounts for {{percentage}}% of your holdings. At ~{{age}} years, your pines are in a strong growth phase. Pine is naturally resistant to many pests, which helps protect your forest.
speciesDescription.birch: Birch represents {{percentage}}% of your forest. At ~{{age}} years, these are relatively young trees. Birch is excellent for biodiversity — it attracts insects, birds, and fungi that strengthen the whole ecosystem.
speciesDescription.oak: Oak makes up {{percentage}}% of your forest. At ~{{age}} years, your oaks are maturing beautifully. Oak forests are among the most biodiverse habitats in Scandinavia and can live for centuries.
speciesDescription.alder: Alder accounts for {{percentage}}% of your forest. At ~{{age}} years, alder plays a special role — it fixes nitrogen in the soil, naturally enriching the ground for neighboring trees.
speciesDescription.beech: Beech represents {{percentage}}% of your forest. At ~{{age}} years, beech provides dense shade and rich leaf litter that builds excellent humus soil over time.
speciesDescription.other: Other species make up {{percentage}}% of your forest, adding to the overall diversity of your holdings.

soilDescription.moraine: Moraine soil holds water well, which means good growth conditions but slightly higher root rot risk. This is the most common forest soil type in Sweden and supports strong timber production.
soilDescription.clay: Clay soil retains moisture effectively, creating lush growing conditions. However, clay can be challenging for machine access during wet periods and increases root rot risk in spruce stands.
soilDescription.sandy_moraine: Sandy moraine drains well and warms up quickly in spring, giving your trees an early start to the growing season. Lower risk of root rot, but may need more attention during dry summers.
soilDescription.peat: Peat soil is rich in organic matter with high water retention. While great for certain species like alder, peat areas may have limited machine accessibility and can indicate wetland habitats worth protecting.
soilDescription.sand: Sandy soil drains quickly and warms up fast. Trees on sandy soil are typically more drought-resistant but may grow somewhat slower. Good conditions for pine.
soilDescription.default: Your {{type}} soil provides the foundation for your forest's growth. Understanding your soil helps predict growth rates, pest risks, and the best management approach.

comparison.birchPercentage: Your forest has {{diff}}% more birch than the regional average ({{value}}% vs {{avg}}%) — this is good for biodiversity and pest resilience.
comparison.speciesCount: You have {{value}} different tree species across your holdings — more diversity than the regional average of {{avg}}, which makes your forest more resilient.
comparison.totalArea: Your forest covers {{value}} ha, which is {{diff}}% larger than the average holding of {{avg}} ha in your region.

trees.title: Your Trees
trees.description: Your forest contains {{count}} different tree species. {{dominant}} dominates at {{dominantPct}}%, shaping the character and value of your holdings.
trees.didYouKnow: A mixed forest with at least 3 species is significantly more resilient against storms, pests, and climate change than a monoculture. Diversity is your forest's insurance policy.
trees.companionPrompt: Tell me more about the tree species in my forest and what I should consider for management.
trees.expandedInfo: Species composition is one of the most important factors in forest management. It affects timber value, biodiversity, pest resistance, and how your forest responds to climate change. The ideal mix depends on your goals — whether that's maximizing timber value, supporting wildlife, or building long-term resilience.

soil.title: Your Soil
soil.didYouKnow: Root rot (rotröta) caused by Heterobasidion is Sweden's most costly forest disease, causing over 1 billion SEK in annual losses. Treating stumps after thinning reduces spread by up to 80%.
soil.companionPrompt: What should I know about my soil type and how it affects forest management?

terrain.title: Your Terrain
terrain.description: Your forest sits at {{elevationAvg}}m elevation ({{elevationMin}}–{{elevationMax}}m range) with {{drainage}} drainage. Machine accessibility is {{machineAccess}} across most areas.
terrain.didYouKnow: Slope and soil bearing capacity determine whether you can use modern harvesters or need cable-based systems. Flat terrain with good drainage keeps harvesting costs 30–40% lower.
terrain.companionPrompt: How does my terrain affect harvesting operations and what should I plan for?

climate.title: Your Climate Zone
climate.description: You're in {{zone}}. Growing season runs {{growingStart}} through {{growingEnd}}, with average summer temperatures around {{summerTemp}}°C and {{precipitation}}mm annual precipitation.
climate.didYouKnow: Every 1°C increase in average temperature extends the growing season by roughly 1 week and shifts the bark beetle flight season earlier. Climate-adapted species selection is becoming increasingly important.
climate.companionPrompt: How is climate change affecting forests in my region and what should I consider?

special.title: What Makes Your Forest Special
special.description: Based on your forest data, we've identified some noteworthy features that make your holdings unique.
special.companionPrompt: Tell me about the special ecological features in my forest and how to protect them.

old_growth.title: Old-Growth Oak Stands
old_growth.description: Your forest includes mature oak stands, which are among Scandinavia's most biodiverse habitats. These trees support hundreds of species of insects, lichens, and fungi — many of which are red-listed. Preserving these areas has both ecological and potential economic value through environmental certifications.

wetland.title: Wetland Areas
wetland.description: Your property includes peat-based wetland areas. These serve as natural water filters, carbon sinks, and habitats for specialized species. Wetlands also help regulate water flow, reducing flood risk downstream. Consider maintaining buffer zones around these areas.

biodiversity.title: High Species Diversity
biodiversity.description: Your forest shows excellent species diversity with multiple well-represented tree species. Mixed forests are more resilient against storms, pests, and drought. This diversity is a valuable asset that many forest owners are actively trying to create.

deadwood.title: Potential Deadwood Habitats
deadwood.description: Higher-elevation areas of your forest likely contain standing and fallen deadwood, which is critical habitat for woodpeckers, beetles, and fungi. Leaving some deadwood during harvesting operations supports biodiversity and is part of FSC certification requirements.

water_proximity.title: Water Features Nearby
water_proximity.description: Your forest is close to water bodies. Maintaining riparian buffer zones protects water quality and provides wildlife corridors. Swedish regulations require setbacks from watercourses during forestry operations.

edge_habitat.title: Forest Edge Habitats
edge_habitat.description: The edges of your forest where it meets open land create unique microhabitats. These transition zones support species from both forest and open-land ecosystems, boosting overall biodiversity.

firstYear.pageSubtitle: A 12-month guided checklist for new forest owners
firstYear.allCompleteMessage: Congratulations! You have completed all tasks in your first year as a forest owner. You are well on your way to becoming an expert forest manager.
firstYear.resetConfirm: Are you sure? This will clear all progress.

professionals.noResultsHint: Try adjusting your filters or search term.

quote.notesPlaceholder: Describe what you need, any special requirements, access conditions, etc.

glossary.title: Forestry Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Search and learn key forestry terminology

simulator.title: Cost & Revenue Simulator
simulator.subtitle: Simulate the economics of forestry operations with current Swedish market prices
simulator.otherIncomeHint: Affects the marginal tax rate when calculating tax on forest income

tax.title: Tax Calculation
tax.subtitle: Estimated tax using Swedish forestry tax rules
tax.skogskontoDesc: Deposit part of your net income into a skogskonto to defer tax. Funds are taxed upon withdrawal.
tax.rantefordelningNote: Interest allocation: {{amount}} taxed as capital income (30%) instead of business income. Savings: {{saved}}
tax.disclaimer: This is an estimate. Always consult your accountant or tax advisor for exact calculations.

explainer.skogskontoBody: Skogskonto allows you to deposit up to 60% of net timber income (max 40% of the property tax assessment value) into a blocked account for up to 10 years. Funds are taxed upon withdrawal, which can lower your marginal tax rate if withdrawn during lower-income years.
explainer.rantefordelningBody: Interest allocation (rantefordelning) means that part of your profit is taxed as capital income (30%) instead of business income (32-57%). The amount is calculated as adjusted equity multiplied by the government bond rate plus 6 percentage points.
explainer.marginalBody: Municipal tax (~32%) applies to all income. State tax (20%) applies to income above ~614,000 SEK, with an additional 5% above ~919,000 SEK. Forest income is added on top of your other income.

deferral.title: Comparison: Harvest now vs. wait 1 year
deferral.explanation: Waiting 1 year adds approximately {{growth}} m³fub in growth and prices are expected to increase by ~{{priceChange}}% based on historical trends.

simulator.disclaimer: Costs based on Skogforsk norms 2026. Timber prices from Q1 2026. Tax calculation is simplified. Not a formal estimate — consult a forestry advisor.

scenarios.pageSubtitle: Explore different forestry scenarios and compare projected outcomes for your forest
scenarios.selectPrompt: Select a scenario to start simulating
scenarios.selectPromptHint: Choose from preset scenarios or build your own
scenarios.talkToAi: Talk to the AI Companion about the best approach for your forest
scenarios.parcelDisclaimer: Based on demo data for a typical 85 ha mixed forest in Småland. Connect your parcels for personalized projections.

doNothing.title: What if I do nothing for 5 years?
doNothing.description: See how your forest evolves with no active management. Competition, aging, and pest pressure increase over time.

thin30.title: What if I thin 30% of my spruce now?
thin30.description: Remove 30% of volume to reduce competition. Remaining trees grow faster, become more wind-stable, and resist beetles better.

beetleUnchecked.title: What if beetle damage spreads unchecked?
beetleUnchecked.description: See the cascading effect of uncontrolled bark beetle spread through your spruce stands over 5 years.

mixedSpecies.title: What if I plant mixed species after harvest?
mixedSpecies.description: Harvest and replant with 40% spruce, 30% pine, 30% broadleaf. Slower start, but vastly better resilience and biodiversity.

climate2c.title: What if climate warms +2°C?
climate2c.description: Model the impact of 2°C warming: extra beetle generations, drought stress on spruce, and shifting growth patterns.

custom.title: Build your own scenario
custom.description: Combine thinning, species selection, climate, and beetle intervention to model your preferred approach.

custom.beetleIntervention: Active beetle intervention (pheromone traps, sanitation felling)

summary.doNothingLoss: In {{years}} years of inaction, your forest loses an estimated {{valueDelta}} kr in timber value as beetle risk increases and health declines.
summary.doNothingGain: Over {{years}} years without intervention, your timber value increases by {{valueDelta}} kr from natural growth, but beetle risk and competition also rise.
summary.thinning: Thinning {{percent}}% of your spruce now increases your forest's value by an estimated {{valueGain}} kr over {{years}} years, while reducing beetle risk and improving growth rates.
summary.beetleUnchecked: If bark beetle damage spreads unchecked, you could lose an estimated {{valueLoss}} kr in timber value over {{years}} years. Sawlog quality drops dramatically in infested wood.
summary.mixedSpecies: Replanting with mixed species costs timber value short-term but boosts biodiversity by {{biodiversityGain}} points over {{years}} years. Your forest becomes far more resilient to pests and climate change.
summary.climate2c: A 2°C warming scenario increases beetle risk by {{riskIncrease}} points and reduces health by {{healthDrop}} points. Southern Swedish spruce forests are particularly vulnerable.
summary.custom: Over {{years}} years, this custom scenario changes your health score by {{healthDelta}} points and timber value by {{valueDelta}} kr compared to doing nothing.

portfolio.title: Portfolio Analytics
portfolio.subtitle: Multi-parcel overview of your forest holdings
portfolio.searchPlaceholder: Search parcels by name, municipality, or county...

market.title: Timber Market
market.subtitle: Real-time timber prices, harvest timing recommendations, and revenue estimation
market.disclaimer: Prices based on Virkesbörsen / Biometria / SDC Q1 2026 data. Not a binding offer. Consult your forest advisor before harvest decisions.

earlyWarning.pageSubtitle: Satellite + drone anomaly detection for bark beetle infestations
earlyWarning.noWarnings: No warnings — your forest looks healthy
earlyWarning.noWarningsDesc: Satellite monitoring is active. You will be notified of any anomalies.
earlyWarning.earlyDetectionMessage: 2-4 weeks before visible symptoms — early intervention can prevent spread

actions.immediateAction: Immediate action required. Arrange sanitation felling and contact Skogsstyrelsen. Order priority drone survey to map exact damage extent.
actions.orderDroneSurvey: Order a drone survey to confirm infestation extent. Prepare contingency plan for sanitation felling if confirmed.
actions.monitorClosely: Monitor closely in next satellite pass. Set up pheromone traps in area. No immediate action needed.
actions.noActionNeeded: No action needed. Continue regular satellite monitoring.

timelineSlider.title: Satellite Timeline
timelineSlider.label: Select week to compare satellite passes

widget.title: Early Warning
widget.healthy: No warnings — your forest looks healthy
widget.healthyDesc: Satellite monitoring active. All NDVI values within normal range.

benchmark.title: Peer Benchmarking
benchmark.subtitle: Anonymous performance comparison for your forest
benchmark.privacyNotice: All comparisons are anonymous. Only aggregated county-level statistics are shown — no individual forest owner data is ever exposed.
benchmark.comparisonChartLabel: Bar chart comparing your parcel with county and national averages
benchmark.widgetRanking: Your forest ranks in the top {{percentile}}% in {{county}}
benchmark.dataSource: Data based on Riksskogstaxeringen, Skogsstyrelsen, and Biometria statistics. Updated quarterly. Percentiles calculated from county-level aggregated data.

page.title: Climate-Adjusted Growth Model
page.subtitle: Site-specific yield projections under climate scenarios

chart.title: Volume Projection
chart.footnote: Projections based on Hägglund & Lundmark yield tables with climate adjustments from SMHI RCP scenarios. Actual growth may vary depending on management, soil conditions, and local microclimate.

rec.species_diversification: Consider diversifying with more drought-tolerant species (birch, oak) to reduce vulnerability

rec.consider_mixed_stands: Convert toward mixed stands with 30-40% deciduous to improve resilience
rec.shorter_rotation: Climate-adjusted growth supports a shorter rotation period — consider harvesting earlier for higher NPV
rec.species_change: Current species may underperform — evaluate transition to more climate-adapted alternatives

page.title: Storm & Windthrow Risk
page.subtitle: Wind vulnerability assessment for your forest parcels

breakdown.meaningCritical: This stand is at critical risk of windthrow. A storm with winds above 20 m/s could cause significant damage. Immediate preventive action is strongly recommended before the next storm season.
breakdown.meaningHigh: This stand has elevated wind vulnerability. Moderate to strong storms could cause partial windthrow, especially along exposed edges. Plan mitigation measures for this season.
breakdown.meaningModerate: This stand has some wind vulnerability factors but is generally stable. Monitor conditions and consider gradual improvements to stand structure over the coming years.
breakdown.meaningLow: This stand has good wind stability. No immediate action needed. Continue routine monitoring and maintain current management approach.

mitigation.subtitle: Priority-ranked actions to reduce wind vulnerability

wind.strongWindBanner: Strong winds forecast — monitor exposed edges and recently thinned stands.

widget.title: Storm Risk

page.title: Carbon Credits & Biodiversity Revenue
page.subtitle: Discover alternative revenue streams from your forest's carbon sequestration and biodiversity value

dashboard.equivalencesDesc: Your annual carbon sequestration is equivalent to offsetting:

revenue.basedOn: Based on {{tons}} ton CO₂/year sequestration across all parcels
revenue.disclaimer: Revenue estimates are indicative. Actual carbon credit pricing depends on market conditions, verification outcomes, and project-specific factors. Consult a certified advisor.

biodiversity.euStrategyDesc: The EU targets 30% of land under protection by 2030. Forests with high biodiversity value may qualify for payments and subsidies.
biodiversity.euEligibleNote: Your forest qualifies for EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 support programs. Contact your county administrative board (Länsstyrelsen) for application details.
biodiversity.lonaDesc: Lokala naturvårdssatsningen (LONA) provides grants for nature conservation, biodiversity enhancement, and outdoor recreation projects.
biodiversity.lonaGrantInfo: LONA grants cover up to 50% of project costs for biodiversity restoration, wetland creation, and recreational trail development on forest land.
biodiversity.certPremiumDesc: Certified sustainable forestry commands premium timber prices. FSC and PEFC certification signal responsible management to buyers.
biodiversity.certPremiumNote: FSC-certified timber typically fetches a 5-15% price premium. PEFC is the most common certification in Sweden, held by ~60% of productive forest land.
biodiversity.setAsideDesc: Of your {{total}} ha total, we recommend setting aside {{recommended}} ha (7%) for biodiversity conservation.
biodiversity.setAsideNote: While set-aside areas reduce harvestable timber, they qualify for environmental subsidies, improve certification standing, and enhance long-term ecosystem resilience.

chart.title: Carbon Pools Over Time
chart.ariaLabel: Stacked area chart showing above-ground, below-ground, and soil carbon pools over time with revenue overlay

wizard.eligibilityDesc: We'll check if your forest parcels meet the basic requirements for carbon credit certification.
wizard.checkAreaPass: Your {{area}} ha exceeds the minimum {{min}} ha requirement
wizard.checkAreaFail: Your {{area}} ha is below the minimum {{min}} ha requirement
wizard.checkAgePass: Average stand age {{age}} years meets minimum requirements
wizard.checkAgeFail: Average stand age {{age}} years may limit eligibility
wizard.checkMgmtDetail: Sustainable management practices required. Review your management plan.
wizard.checkAdditionalityDetail: Projects must demonstrate carbon sequestration beyond business-as-usual. Young plantations strengthen this case.
wizard.allChecksPass: All eligibility checks passed. You can proceed to program comparison.
wizard.someChecksFail: Some checks need attention. You may still proceed, but address flagged items.
wizard.docsDesc: These documents are typically required for carbon credit certification. Check off items you already have.
wizard.doc_forestInventory_desc: Recent inventory with species, age, volume, and site index
wizard.doc_baselineAssessment_desc: Historical carbon stock estimate (can be modeled)
wizard.doc_monitoringPlan_desc: How carbon stocks will be measured and verified over time
wizard.doc_stakeholderConsultation_desc: Documentation of community and stakeholder engagement
wizard.doc_environmentalImpact_desc: Assessment of project's impact on local biodiversity and water
wizard.timelineEstimate: Estimated {{months}} months from start to first credit issuance
wizard.connectDesc: These organizations operate in the Swedish market and can guide you through the certification process.
wizard.nextSteps: Ready to take the next step?
wizard.nextStepsDesc: Contact a certifier for a free initial consultation. They can provide a detailed project assessment and timeline specific to your forest.

advisor.pageSubtitle: Expert-grade, citation-backed AI advice for forest management decisions

advisor.noCitations: No specific citations available for custom questions. Use a standard decision type for citation-backed analysis.
advisor.noDecisionsDesc: Run an analysis and save it to track your forest management decisions over time.

decisions.thinningDesc: Evaluate thinning intensity, timing, and financial returns (gallring)
decisions.finalFellingDesc: Optimal rotation age and harvest timing analysis (slutavverkning)
decisions.regenerationDesc: Species selection after harvest for climate-adapted regeneration (föryngring)
decisions.continuousCoverDesc: Evaluate transition to uneven-aged forestry (hyggesfritt skogsbruk)
decisions.beetleDamageDesc: Sanitation harvest strategy for bark beetle infestation (granbarkborre)
decisions.pctDesc: Investment analysis for young stand cleaning (röjning)
decisions.customDesc: Ask any forestry management question about your stand

mitigation.beetle: Maintain stand vitality through timely thinning. Remove windthrown and stressed trees promptly.
mitigation.windthrow: Avoid thinning during storm season. Favor gradual removals over heavy single interventions.
mitigation.priceVolatility: Consider forward contracts or staggered harvesting to reduce market timing risk.
mitigation.climate: Diversify species mix. Consider drought-tolerant alternatives for regeneration.
mitigation.browsing: Apply mechanical protection or chemical repellent on pine regeneration in high-moose-density areas.
mitigation.longHorizon: Sensitivity to discount rate is high for long-rotation decisions. Consider alternative rates.
mitigation.ccfOperations: Engage experienced CCF operators. Start with trial blocks before full conversion.
mitigation.naturalRegen: Supplement with planting in gaps where natural regeneration fails after 2-3 years.
mitigation.beetleUrgent: URGENT: Remove infested timber within 3 weeks. Debark or transport to mill immediately.
mitigation.blueStain: Prioritize rapid extraction. Negotiate salvage prices with mills before harvest.
mitigation.neighborSpread: Coordinate with neighboring forest owners. Report to Skogsstyrelsen if required.

page.title: Succession & Estate Planning
page.subtitle: Tax-optimized generational transfer planning for your forest
page.heroDescription: Sweden abolished gift tax and inheritance tax in 2004. With the right planning, you can transfer your forest to the next generation with minimal tax impact. This tool helps you compare methods, split value fairly, and prepare all required documents.
page.disclaimer: This tool provides general guidance based on Swedish tax rules as of 2024. It is not legal or tax advice. Always consult a qualified tax advisor or lawyer specializing in forest property transfers before making decisions. Tax rules and assessed values change — verify current rates with Skatteverket.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.body: Calculations use the Faustmann/LEV formula with SLU Heureka yield tables for Swedish conditions. Timber prices from Skogforsk 2024 averages. Carbon credit pricing based on voluntary market mid-range. Hunting lease rates from Jagarefoerbundet benchmarks for southern Sweden. Biodiversity payments from LONA/EU subsidy schedules. All results are estimates for planning purposes.

q1.question: How does BeetleSense detect bark beetle infestations?
q1.answer: We analyze multispectral satellite imagery (Sentinel-2) to detect changes in vegetation health indices like NDVI and moisture content. Our AI models, trained on thousands of confirmed infestations in Swedish forests, can identify stress patterns in spruce stands 2-4 weeks before visible symptoms appear. For higher resolution, drone surveys provide centimeter-level detail.

q2.question: Which satellite data do you use?
q2.answer: We primarily use Copernicus Sentinel-2 imagery, which provides free 10m resolution multispectral data every 5 days. For Pro and Enterprise users, we also integrate data from Lantmäteriet's laser scanning (LiDAR), SMHI weather data, and Skogsstyrelsen's open forest datasets.

q3.question: Is my forest data secure?
q3.answer: Yes. All data is stored in EU-based servers compliant with GDPR. Your parcel boundaries and forest data are encrypted and never shared with third parties. You retain full ownership of all data you upload. We are also compliant with Skogsstyrelsen's data handling guidelines.

q4.question: Do I need to install anything?
q4.answer: No. BeetleSense is a web application that works in any modern browser on desktop, tablet, or phone. It's also a Progressive Web App (PWA) — you can install it on your home screen for app-like experience and offline access in the field.

q5.question: How accurate are the AI detections?
q5.answer: Our satellite-based early warning system achieves approximately 87% accuracy for bark beetle detection in spruce stands, validated against Skogsstyrelsen ground truth data. Drone surveys increase accuracy to over 95%. We continuously improve our models with new training data from Swedish forests.

q6.question: Can I use BeetleSense offline in the forest?
q6.answer: Yes. Field Mode allows you to download maps and parcel data before heading into the forest. You can capture photos, GPS coordinates, and observations offline. Everything syncs automatically when you regain connectivity.

q7.question: What forest types does BeetleSense support?
q7.answer: BeetleSense is optimized for Swedish boreal forests, including spruce (gran), pine (tall), and mixed stands. Our models are specifically trained on Nordic conditions. We support all forest types registered with Lantmäteriet across Sweden.

q8.question: Can I cancel my subscription anytime?
q8.answer: Yes. You can cancel your Pro subscription at any time. You'll retain access until the end of your current billing period. Your data remains available for export for 90 days after cancellation. The free tier is always available with no time limits.
